directions.name: Restore accepted base, then retime refill issue order
directions.hypothesis: Anchor on the latest measured run 20260420_085928_bf16_gemm_v1_9ee9b48 at 25.821696 ms, restore the accepted grouped_rows=8 base with reversed PTX row-pair traversal, right-left PTX column sweep, and the one-sync wait_group_0 handoff, then try a narrow steady-state refill issue-order retime inside that accepted base. The mirrored-sweep experiment already reduced long scoreboard to 4.48 but still regressed badly, so consumer ordering is no longer the best next lever.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Refill issue ordering after the accepted one-sync handoff, not the consumer sweep order itself.
directions.risk: Low-to-moderate: if the accepted base is not fully restored first, a refill retime can confound the signal and drift back into the rejected mirrored or split-sweep paths.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the accepted grouped_rows=8 base is restored but a local refill issue-order retime does not improve time without reopening the rejected mirrored or split-sweep branches.

directions.name: Minimal overlap recovery behind the one-sync handoff
directions.hypothesis: Once the accepted grouped_rows=8 base and one-sync handoff are restored, there may still be a small overlap gap between consumer drain and producer refill. Try a minimal overlap-recovery experiment behind that handoff only, keeping the consumer order fixed and avoiding any reopen of the full mirrored or split sweep logic.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual overlap loss in the steady-state handoff window after the accepted consumer path has already drained.
directions.risk: Medium: overlap changes can easily overfit timing and recreate the rejected warmup-order or broader pipeline-shape behavior.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the overlap tweak needs any consumer-order reopen, extra syncs, or a wider pipeline rewrite to show benefit.

directions.name: Very local consumer-order closure only
directions.hypothesis: If the accepted base and refill-retime paths do not recover the loss, a very local consumer-order closure may still help, but only if it keeps both the split sweep and the full mirrored sweep explicitly closed. This is a last-mile path, not a renewed structural sweep experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual consumer-side locality loss in the hot-band PTX sweep, limited to a small ordering closure.
directions.risk: High: the allowed surface is very narrow, and any drift toward the split sweep, mirrored sweep, grouped_rows=16, or other rejected families should be treated as out of bounds.
directions.stop_condition: Stop after one very local closure trial and do not broaden into split-sweep or full mirrored-sweep variants.

notes: Anchored to the latest measured run 20260420_085928_bf16_gemm_v1_9ee9b48 at 25.821696 ms. Keep the accepted grouped_rows=8 base, reversed PTX row-pair traversal, right-left PTX column sweep, and one-sync wait_group_0 handoff as the primary baseline; keep split sweep, full mirrored sweep, grouped_rows=16, warmup-order reopen, K32 cadence, extra-live B lookahead, unroll-1 base, CTA-level B repack, and broad shared-memory rewrites closed for this round.
